name: He bore our griefs
notes: Isaiah 53 is the great Suffering Servant prophecy, and the New Testament reaches back to it again and again to declare it fulfilled in Christ. The chapter opens lamenting that few would believe the report, which both John and Paul quote of those who rejected the gospel; it foretells the Servant bearing griefs and sicknesses, which Matthew applies to Jesus' healing ministry; and it describes the wounding, the stripes, the sinlessness, and the bearing of the sin of many, which Peter gathers up as accomplished at the cross. Each evangelist and apostle keeps his own vantage: John and Paul on Israel's unbelief, Matthew on bodily healing, Peter on the atonement and the believer's call to righteousness. Together they show one prophecy unfolding across the whole work of Christ. The shared wording, often word-for-word with the prophet, is left as the visible thread binding promise to fulfillment.

links.label: who hath believed our report ... the arm of the Lord
Lord who hath believed our report
borne our griefs ... bare our sicknesses
with his stripes we are healed ... by whose stripes ye were healed
he bare the sin of many ... his own self bare our sins
